items.name: Jericho (Tell es-Sultan)
items.period: Neolithic to Iron Age (9000 BC - 586 BC)
items.description: One of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. Multiple layers of settlement show repeated destruction and rebuilding over thousands of years.
items.significance: Evidence of a massive destruction layer with walls that fell outward (unusual - walls normally fall inward when attacked). City was burned after walls collapsed. Stored grain found in houses suggests sudden destruction rather than prolonged siege.

key_finds.name: Collapsed mudbrick walls
key_finds.details: Walls found fallen outward at base of tell, providing a ramp into the city

key_finds.name: Burned destruction layer
key_finds.details: Thick ash layer covering the city, indicating fire followed the collapse

key_finds.name: Full grain storage jars
key_finds.details: Unusual - invaders normally take food supplies. Suggests quick destruction

items.biblical_connection: Joshua's conquest describes walls falling flat (Jos 6:20), city burned with fire (Jos 6:24), and command not to take plunder (Jos 6:17-19)
items.scholarly_notes: Dating debated. Garstang dated destruction to ~1400 BC (fits early Exodus date). Kenyon dated it to ~1550 BC (too early). Recent analysis by Bryant Wood suggests Garstang may have been closer.
items.current_location: Site open to visitors; artifacts in Rockefeller Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Megiddo (Tel Megiddo)
items.discovered: 1903-05 (Gottlieb Schumacher); major excavations 1925-39 (Oriental Institute of Chicago)
items.description: Strategic fortress city controlling the Via Maris trade route. Over 20 layers of occupation show its importance across millennia. One of Solomon's chariot cities.
items.significance: Massive gate complex matches gates at Hazor and Gezer - all three attributed to Solomon (1Ki 9:15). Evidence of Canaanite temples, Egyptian influence, and Israelite fortifications.

key_finds.name: Six-chambered gate
key_finds.details: Identical design found at Hazor and Gezer, matching the three cities Solomon fortified

key_finds.name: Stable complexes
key_finds.details: Large structures originally identified as Solomon's stables; now debated

key_finds.name: Water tunnel system
key_finds.details: Underground tunnel to spring, similar to Jerusalem's water systems

items.biblical_connection: Listed as Solomon's fortification project (1Ki 9:15). Site of battles including Deborah's victory (Jdg 5:19) and Josiah's death fighting Pharaoh Necho (2Ki 23:29-30). Greek name 'Armageddon' from Hebrew Har-Megiddo (Rev 16:16).
items.scholarly_notes: Gate dating debated - some attribute to Solomon, others to Ahab. Multiple destruction layers confirm strategic importance.
items.current_location: Tel Megiddo National Park; artifacts in Israel Museum and Oriental Institute, Chicago

items.name: Hazor (Tel Hazor)
items.description: Largest Canaanite city in the land, called 'the head of all those kingdoms' (Jos 11:10). At its peak covered 200 acres with population of 40,000. Major destruction layer shows violent burning.
items.significance: Massive destruction layer dated to 13th century BC shows city was burned with intense fire - Canaanite statues found deliberately broken. Rebuilt much smaller under Solomon.

key_finds.name: Burned destruction layer
key_finds.details: Thick ash and debris showing complete destruction by fire

key_finds.name: Mutilated statues
key_finds.details: Canaanite religious statues found with heads and hands deliberately broken off

key_finds.name: Six-chambered gate
key_finds.details: Same design as Megiddo and Gezer, attributed to Solomon

items.biblical_connection: Joshua burned Hazor after defeating King Jabin (Jos 11:10-13). Later rebuilt and fortified by Solomon (1Ki 9:15). Another King Jabin oppressed Israel in Judges period (Jdg 4:2).
items.scholarly_notes: Only Hazor said to be burned by Joshua among northern cities (Jos 11:13). Archaeological evidence matches this detail precisely.
items.current_location: Tel Hazor National Park; artifacts in Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: City of David (Jerusalem)
items.description: Original Jerusalem captured by David from the Jebusites. Small ridge south of Temple Mount, only 12 acres. Water shaft discovered explains how David's men entered the city.
items.significance: Warren's Shaft and Hezekiah's Tunnel show advanced water engineering. Large Stone Structure may be David's palace or Jebusite fortress (the Millo). Hundreds of seal impressions from royal officials found.

key_finds.name: Warren's Shaft
key_finds.details: Water system that may be the 'water shaft' David's men used to take the city (2Sa 5:8)

key_finds.name: Hezekiah's Tunnel
key_finds.details: 533-meter tunnel bringing Gihon Spring water inside walls before Assyrian siege

key_finds.name: Large Stone Structure
key_finds.details: Massive Iron Age building, possibly David's palace or Jebusite fortress

key_finds.name: Bullae (seal impressions)
key_finds.details: Over 50 clay seal impressions naming biblical officials including Baruch son of Neriah (Jeremiah's scribe)

items.biblical_connection: David captured Jebus and made it his capital (2Sa 5:6-9). Solomon built temple on threshing floor above (2Ch 3:1). Hezekiah's tunnel prepared for Assyrian siege (2Ki 20:20).
items.scholarly_notes: Debate over extent of 10th century Jerusalem. Recent finds suggest larger settlement than minimalists proposed.
items.current_location: City of David Archaeological Park, Jerusalem

items.name: Samaria (Sebaste)
items.description: Capital of northern kingdom of Israel, founded by King Omri. Built on a hill he purchased for two talents of silver. Later expanded by his son Ahab with ivory-decorated palace.
items.significance: Ivory fragments confirm the 'ivory house' Ahab built (1Ki 22:39). Ostraca (pottery inscriptions) mention tax payments and places. City fell to Assyria in 722 BC.

key_finds.name: Ivory carvings
key_finds.details: Hundreds of ivory fragments from furniture and wall panels, confirming Ahab's ivory house

key_finds.name: Samaria Ostraca
key_finds.details: 63 pottery inscriptions recording shipments of wine and oil, showing administrative system

key_finds.name: Royal acropolis
key_finds.details: Omri and Ahab's palace complex with fine ashlar masonry

items.biblical_connection: Omri bought the hill and built Samaria (1Ki 16:24). Ahab added ivory house (1Ki 22:39). Prophets condemned its luxury (Amo 3:15). Fell to Assyria (2Ki 17:5-6).
items.scholarly_notes: Quality of construction confirms biblical description of wealthy capital. Ostraca provide insight into daily administration.
items.current_location: Sebastia Archaeological Park; artifacts in Israel Museum and Istanbul Museum

items.name: Capernaum (Kfar Nahum)
items.description: Fishing village that became Jesus' home base during his Galilean ministry. Called 'his own city' (Mat 9:1). Synagogue and Peter's house identified.
items.significance: White limestone synagogue (4th century) built over black basalt synagogue from Jesus' time - likely where Jesus taught. House church built over traditional site of Peter's house shows early Christian veneration.

key_finds.name: Basalt synagogue foundation
key_finds.details: 1st century synagogue beneath later limestone synagogue, likely where Jesus taught

key_finds.name: Peter's house
key_finds.details: 1st century house with later church built over it; graffiti mentions Jesus and Peter

key_finds.name: Fishing equipment
key_finds.details: Anchors, hooks, and weights confirming fishing industry

items.biblical_connection: Jesus made Capernaum his base (Mat 4:13). Healed in synagogue (Mar 1:21-28). Peter's house nearby (Mar 1:29-31). Pronounced judgment on unbelieving city (Mat 11:23-24).
items.scholarly_notes: Octagonal church over Peter's house shows veneration began in 1st century. Many miracles recorded here.
items.current_location: Capernaum National Park; managed by Franciscans

items.name: Bethsaida (et-Tell)
items.description: Fishing village on north shore of Sea of Galilee. Home of apostles Peter, Andrew, and Philip. Upgraded to city status by Herod Philip, who renamed it Julias.
items.significance: Iron Age city gate and temple found. Roman-era fishing village matches gospel description. One of cities Jesus condemned for unbelief despite his miracles there.

key_finds.name: City gate complex
key_finds.details: Iron Age gate showing city's importance in Old Testament period

key_finds.name: Fishermen's houses
key_finds.details: 1st century domestic structures with fishing equipment

key_finds.name: Wine cellar
key_finds.details: Roman period storage facility

items.biblical_connection: Home of Peter, Andrew, and Philip (Joh 1:44). Jesus healed blind man here (Mar 8:22-26). Fed 5,000 nearby (Luk 9:10-17). Condemned for unbelief (Mat 11:21).
items.scholarly_notes: Location debated - et-Tell is about 2km from current shoreline due to sediment. Some propose el-Araj closer to water.
items.current_location: Bethsaida Excavations Park; artifacts in Israel Museum

items.name: Lachish (Tel Lachish)
items.description: Second most important city in Judah after Jerusalem. Massive fortifications and palace-fort. Destroyed by Sennacherib in 701 BC; siege depicted in Assyrian palace reliefs.
items.significance: Assyrian siege ramp still visible. Lachish Letters written during Babylonian invasion preserve desperate military communications from final days of Judah.

key_finds.name: Assyrian siege ramp
key_finds.details: Massive earth and stone ramp Sennacherib built to attack city walls

key_finds.name: Counter-ramp
key_finds.details: Defenders built ramp inside wall to maintain height advantage

key_finds.name: Lachish Letters
key_finds.details: 21 ostraca from Babylonian siege, military correspondence mentioning fire signals

key_finds.name: Mass grave
key_finds.details: 1,500 bodies in cave, likely siege casualties

items.biblical_connection: Sennacherib besieged Lachish while demanding Jerusalem's surrender (2Ki 18:13-17). Jeremiah mentions Lachish as one of last cities standing (Jer 34:7).
items.scholarly_notes: Lachish relief in British Museum shows siege in detail. Letters mention prophet possibly referring to Jeremiah.
items.current_location: Tel Lachish National Park; letters in Israel Museum; reliefs in British Museum

items.name: Gezer (Tel Gezer)
items.description: Strategic city guarding route from coast to Jerusalem. Given to Solomon by Pharaoh as dowry for his daughter. One of three cities Solomon fortified.
items.significance: Six-chambered gate identical to Megiddo and Hazor confirms Solomon's building program. Gezer Calendar is one of oldest Hebrew inscriptions.

key_finds.name: Six-chambered gate
key_finds.details: Same design as Megiddo and Hazor gates, attributed to Solomon

key_finds.name: Gezer Calendar
key_finds.details: 10th century BC inscription listing agricultural seasons - oldest Hebrew writing

key_finds.name: High place
key_finds.details: Ten standing stones in row, Canaanite worship site

key_finds.name: Water tunnel
key_finds.details: Underground system reaching water table

items.biblical_connection: Pharaoh conquered Gezer and gave it to Solomon as dowry (1Ki 9:16). Solomon rebuilt it along with Hazor and Megiddo (1Ki 9:15).
items.scholarly_notes: Gezer Calendar shows agricultural knowledge. Boundary stones mark city limits with 'belonging to Gezer' in Hebrew and Greek.
items.current_location: Tel Gezer National Park; calendar in Istanbul Archaeological Museum

items.name: Dan (Tel Dan)
items.location: Northern tip of Israel, Golan Heights base
items.description: Northernmost city of Israel ('from Dan to Beersheba'). Originally called Laish, captured by tribe of Dan. Jeroboam set up golden calf here to rival Jerusalem temple.
items.significance: Mud-brick gate from Abraham's time preserved. Iron Age gate complex where kings held court. Tel Dan Stele provides first extra-biblical mention of 'House of David'.

key_finds.name: Middle Bronze Age gate
key_finds.details: 4,000-year-old mud-brick arch gate, oldest arch in world, from Abraham's era

key_finds.name: Iron Age gate complex
key_finds.details: Where kings sat in judgment; throne platform and canopy bases found

key_finds.name: Tel Dan Stele
key_finds.details: 9th century inscription mentioning 'House of David' - first extra-biblical reference

key_finds.name: High place
key_finds.details: Large platform where Jeroboam's golden calf likely stood

items.biblical_connection: Abraham pursued kings to Dan (Gen 14:14). Tribe of Dan captured Laish (Jdg 18:27-29). Jeroboam set up calf worship (1Ki 12:28-30).
items.scholarly_notes: Tel Dan Stele silenced claims that David was legendary. Date matches time of Hazael of Damascus.
items.current_location: Tel Dan Nature Reserve; stele in Israel Museum

items.name: Nineveh (Kuyunjik)
items.description: Capital of Assyrian Empire at its height under Sennacherib and Ashurbanipal. Massive walls, palaces, and library. Destroyed in 612 BC as prophesied by Nahum.
items.significance: Sennacherib's palace contained reliefs showing Lachish siege and records claiming 200,150 Jews deported. Library of Ashurbanipal preserved 30,000 cuneiform tablets.

key_finds.name: Sennacherib's palace
key_finds.details: Southwest Palace with 71 rooms, many with carved reliefs

key_finds.name: Lachish reliefs
key_finds.details: Detailed stone carvings of Assyrian siege of Lachish

key_finds.name: Taylor Prism
key_finds.details: Sennacherib's account of Jerusalem campaign - 'shut up Hezekiah like a bird in a cage'

key_finds.name: Library of Ashurbanipal
key_finds.details: 30,000+ tablets including Epic of Gilgamesh with flood account

items.biblical_connection: Jonah preached repentance there (Jon 3:1-10). Nahum prophesied destruction (Nah 1-3). Sennacherib ruled from here during invasion of Judah (2Ki 19:36).
items.scholarly_notes: Fall in 612 BC fulfilled Nahum's prophecy. Library tablets revolutionized understanding of ancient Near East.
items.current_location: Site in Iraq (limited access); artifacts in British Museum and Iraq Museum

items.name: Babylon
items.description: Capital of Babylonian Empire. Nebuchadnezzar II made it largest city in world with famous walls, Ishtar Gate, and legendary hanging gardens. Jews exiled here 586 BC.
items.significance: Ishtar Gate and Processional Way show city's grandeur. Administrative tablets mention King Jehoiachin of Judah receiving rations. Walls match Greek descriptions.

key_finds.name: Ishtar Gate
key_finds.details: Blue glazed brick gate with dragons and bulls, reconstructed in Berlin

key_finds.name: Processional Way
key_finds.details: Street leading to temples where New Year festival held

key_finds.name: Jehoiachin tablets
key_finds.details: Ration lists naming 'Yaukin king of Judah' (Jehoiachin) receiving food allotments

key_finds.name: Etemenanki ziggurat
key_finds.details: Base of massive stepped tower, possibly inspiration for Tower of Babel

items.biblical_connection: Jews exiled to Babylon (2Ki 25:8-11). Nebuchadnezzar's madness (Dan 4). Belshazzar's feast (Dan 5). Prophets condemned (Isa 13-14, Jer 50-51).
items.scholarly_notes: Jehoiachin tablets confirm biblical account of king's survival in exile (2Ki 25:27-30). City fell to Persians in 539 BC as Daniel records.
items.current_location: Site in Iraq; Ishtar Gate in Pergamon Museum, Berlin; tablets in British Museum

items.name: Ur (Tell el-Muqayyar)
items.description: Abraham's hometown before God called him to Canaan. Major Sumerian city with massive ziggurat to moon god. Royal tombs showed advanced civilization.
items.significance: Ziggurat of Ur-Nammu stands 70 feet high. Royal Cemetery had gold, jewelry, and servants buried with kings. Shows sophisticated culture Abraham left behind.

key_finds.name: Great Ziggurat
key_finds.details: Three-stage stepped tower dedicated to moon god Nanna, partially restored

key_finds.name: Royal Cemetery
key_finds.details: Tombs with gold artifacts, lyres, gaming boards, and sacrificed attendants

key_finds.name: Ur-Nammu law code
key_finds.details: Law code predating Hammurabi by 300 years

key_finds.name: Residential houses
key_finds.details: Two-story homes with courtyards showing urban lifestyle

items.biblical_connection: Abraham came from Ur of the Chaldeans (Gen 11:31). God called him to leave for Canaan (Gen 12:1). Stephen mentions Ur in his speech (Act 7:2-4).
items.scholarly_notes: Debate over which Ur - southern Mesopotamian city most accepted. Abraham left advanced urban culture for nomadic life.
items.current_location: Site in Iraq (US military base nearby); artifacts in British Museum, Penn Museum, Iraq Museum

items.name: Shiloh (Khirbet Seilun)
items.description: Israel's religious center before Jerusalem. Tabernacle stood here for 369 years. Samuel grew up serving at Shiloh. Destroyed, likely by Philistines after Ark captured.
items.significance: Large platform cut into rock may be where tabernacle stood. Destruction layer dated to mid-11th century BC matches time of Eli and Samuel. City never fully recovered.

key_finds.name: Rock-cut platform
key_finds.details: Level area that could have supported tabernacle, matching dimensions

key_finds.name: Destruction debris
key_finds.details: Burned layer from ~1050 BC, time of Philistine wars

key_finds.name: Storage rooms
key_finds.details: Rooms possibly for tabernacle supplies or sacrificial offerings

key_finds.name: Byzantine church
key_finds.details: Later church commemorating tabernacle site

items.biblical_connection: Tabernacle set up at Shiloh (Jos 18:1). Hannah prayed for Samuel there (1Sa 1:9-11). Ark captured by Philistines (1Sa 4:1-11). Jeremiah warns Jerusalem will become like Shiloh (Jer 7:12-14).
items.scholarly_notes: Jeremiah's reference shows Shiloh's destruction was well-known. Site confirms destruction but Philistine involvement is inference.
items.current_location: Tel Shiloh Archaeological Park; artifacts in Israel Museum

items.name: Qumran
items.discovered: 1947 (Bedouin shepherds found scrolls); excavations 1951-56 (Roland de Vaux)
items.description: Settlement of Jewish community (likely Essenes) who copied and preserved scrolls. Scriptorium, ritual baths, and communal facilities found. Destroyed by Romans in 68 AD.
items.significance: Community that hid Dead Sea Scrolls. Provides insight into Jewish life and beliefs in Jesus' time. Shows careful scripture preservation.

key_finds.name: Scriptorium
key_finds.details: Room with tables and inkwells where scrolls were copied

key_finds.name: Ritual baths (mikva'ot)
key_finds.details: Multiple stepped pools for ritual purification

key_finds.name: Cemetery
key_finds.details: 1,200+ graves aligned north-south, mostly adult males

key_finds.name: Pottery workshop
key_finds.details: Where scroll jars were likely manufactured

items.biblical_connection: Community preserved Old Testament texts 1,000 years older than previous manuscripts. Shows scripture unchanged over millennia.
items.scholarly_notes: Debate whether Qumran residents wrote scrolls or just stored them. Essene connection widely accepted but not certain.
items.current_location: Qumran National Park; scrolls in Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum

items.name: Tel Dan Stele
items.location: Found at Tel Dan, northern Israel
items.description: Broken basalt monument set up by Aramean king (likely Hazael of Damascus) boasting of military victories. Contains phrase 'House of David' - first reference to David outside the Bible.
items.significance: Silenced scholarly claims that David was legendary or mythical. Written by Israel's enemy, making mention of David's dynasty more credible.

key_finds.name: Fragment A
key_finds.details: Main piece with 'House of David' phrase

key_finds.name: Fragments B1 and B2
key_finds.details: Additional pieces found nearby in 1994

items.biblical_connection: References 'king of Israel' and 'House of David' (king of Judah). Likely describes events from 2 Kings 8-9 involving Hazael's wars against Israel and Judah.
items.scholarly_notes: Some scholars initially questioned reading but consensus now accepts 'House of David' interpretation. Changes debate about historicity of David.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Siloam Inscription
items.description: Hebrew inscription carved in tunnel wall celebrating completion of Hezekiah's water tunnel. Describes two teams of workers digging from opposite ends and meeting in middle.
items.significance: Confirms 2 Kings 20:20 account of Hezekiah's tunnel construction. Shows Hebrew script and language of 8th century BC. Engineering feat - 533 meters through solid rock.

key_finds.name: Six-line inscription
key_finds.details: Classical Hebrew describing tunnel completion

items.biblical_connection: Hezekiah made the pool and tunnel to bring water into city before Assyrian siege (2Ki 20:20, 2Ch 32:30).
items.scholarly_notes: Inscription cut from wall illegally in 1890; now in Istanbul. Tunnel exactly as described - teams met slightly off-center.
items.current_location: Istanbul Archaeological Museum (tunnel remains in Jerusalem)

items.name: Cyrus Cylinder
items.description: Clay cylinder inscribed by Persian King Cyrus the Great after conquering Babylon. Describes his policy of allowing exiled peoples to return home and rebuild temples.
items.significance: Confirms Cyrus's decree allowing exiles to return. Shows his policy was empire-wide, not special treatment for Jews. Called 'first charter of human rights' (though this is modern interpretation).

key_finds.name: Cylinder text
key_finds.details: 45 lines in Akkadian cuneiform describing Cyrus's policies

items.biblical_connection: Cyrus decreed Jews could return and rebuild temple (Ezr 1:1-4). Isaiah prophesied Cyrus by name 150 years earlier (Isa 44:28, 45:1). Called God's 'shepherd' and 'anointed.'
items.scholarly_notes: Cylinder shows Cyrus presented himself as liberator. Jewish sources (Ezra, Isaiah) and Persian record agree on his character.
items.current_location: British Museum, London (replica at UN headquarters)

items.name: Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
items.description: Black limestone obelisk showing tribute brought to Assyrian king Shalmaneser III from conquered peoples. Contains earliest image of an Israelite - King Jehu bowing down.
items.significance: Only ancient image of an Israelite king. Shows Jehu (or his ambassador) prostrated before Assyrian king, paying tribute. Confirms Jehu's dynasty and Assyrian dominance.

key_finds.name: Jehu panel
key_finds.details: Shows figure bowing with caption 'tribute of Jehu son of Omri'

key_finds.name: Five registers
key_finds.details: Multiple panels showing tribute from different nations

items.biblical_connection: Jehu became king by coup (2Ki 9-10). Paying tribute to Assyria not mentioned in Bible but shows political situation. Called 'son of Omri' (dynasty name, not literal).
items.scholarly_notes: Jehu destroyed Omri's dynasty but Assyrians still called Israel 'house of Omri.' Shows international recognition of Omride power.
items.current_location: British Museum, London

items.name: Taylor Prism (Sennacherib's Prism)
items.description: Six-sided clay prism with Assyrian king Sennacherib's account of his military campaigns. Describes siege of Jerusalem - 'I shut up Hezekiah the Jew like a bird in a cage.'
items.significance: Confirms 2 Kings 18-19 account from enemy perspective. Sennacherib boasts of conquests but notably does NOT claim to have taken Jerusalem - matches biblical account of miraculous deliverance.

key_finds.name: Jerusalem campaign account
key_finds.details: Claims 46 walled cities taken, 200,150 people deported, but no capture of Jerusalem

items.biblical_connection: Sennacherib invaded Judah in Hezekiah's 14th year (2Ki 18:13). Besieged Jerusalem but angel killed 185,000 Assyrians (2Ki 19:35). Sennacherib returned home without taking city.
items.scholarly_notes: Assyrian kings never admitted defeats. Sennacherib's silence about taking Jerusalem is significant - it agrees with biblical account of his failure there.
items.current_location: British Museum, London

items.name: Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone)
items.discovered: 1868 (F.A. Klein); broken by Bedouin, partially reconstructed
items.description: Black basalt monument by Moabite King Mesha celebrating victory over Israel. Mentions Omri, Israel, and possibly 'House of David.' Oldest inscription mentioning Israelite god YHWH.
items.significance: Confirms Omri's oppression of Moab and Moabite revolt. Provides Moabite perspective on events in 2 Kings 3. May contain second 'House of David' reference.

key_finds.name: 34-line inscription
key_finds.details: Moabite language (similar to Hebrew) describing Mesha's victories

items.biblical_connection: Mesha king of Moab rebelled after Ahab's death (2Ki 3:4-5). Israel, Judah, and Edom attacked but withdrew (2Ki 3:6-27). Stele gives Moab's victory claim.
items.scholarly_notes: Stele broken when Bedouin heated and poured water on it. Squeeze (paper impression) made earlier preserves text. 'House of David' reading debated but increasingly accepted.
items.current_location: Louvre Museum, Paris

items.name: Ketef Hinnom Scrolls
items.description: Two tiny silver scrolls found in burial cave, worn as amulets. When unrolled, found to contain oldest known text of scripture - the priestly blessing from Numbers 6:24-26.
items.significance: 400 years older than Dead Sea Scrolls. Shows biblical text was fixed and revered before Babylonian exile. Text matches Masoretic tradition almost exactly.

key_finds.name: KH1 (larger scroll)
key_finds.details: Contains expanded version of priestly blessing and divine name

key_finds.name: KH2 (smaller scroll)
key_finds.details: Contains Numbers 6:24-26 blessing

items.biblical_connection: Contains priestly blessing: 'The LORD bless you and keep you; the LORD make his face shine upon you...' (Num 6:24-26)
items.scholarly_notes: Careful unrolling at Israel Museum took three years. Proves biblical text existed in written form before exile.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Pilate Stone
items.description: Limestone block with Latin inscription mentioning Pontius Pilate as prefect of Judea. Part of dedication for building (possibly temple to Tiberius). Only archaeological evidence of Pilate.
items.significance: Confirms Pilate's existence, title, and time period. Corrects assumption he was 'procurator' - stone shows he was 'prefect.' First physical evidence of gospel character.

key_finds.name: Four-line inscription
key_finds.details: Partially readable: '[Pon]tius Pilatus [Praef]ectus Iuda[ea]e'

items.biblical_connection: Pilate judged Jesus and ordered his crucifixion (Mat 27:11-26, Mar 15:1-15, Luk 23:1-25, Joh 18:28-19:16). Gospels accurately place him as governor of Judea under Tiberius.
items.scholarly_notes: Stone reused as theater seat, which preserved it. Original building unknown. Confirms gospel chronology.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem (replica at Caesarea)

items.name: Caiaphas Ossuary
items.description: Ornate bone box (ossuary) inscribed 'Joseph son of Caiaphas.' Contained bones of 60-year-old man. From family tomb with 12 ossuaries. High priest who tried Jesus.
items.significance: Confirms existence of high priest Caiaphas. Shows his full name was Joseph Caiaphas (Josephus calls him 'Joseph who was called Caiaphas'). Proves wealthy priestly status.

key_finds.name: Inscribed ossuary
key_finds.details: Decorated box with 'Yehosef bar Qafa' (Joseph son of Caiaphas) in Aramaic

key_finds.name: Bones
key_finds.details: Remains of elderly man, possibly the high priest himself

items.biblical_connection: Caiaphas was high priest who tried Jesus (Mat 26:3, 57). Prophesied Jesus would die for the nation (Joh 11:49-52). Led prosecution before Sanhedrin.
items.scholarly_notes: Some scholars question identification, but name, location, time period, and wealth indicators strongly support connection to gospel Caiaphas.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Pool of Siloam
items.description: Large stepped pool at end of Hezekiah's Tunnel. Used for ritual purification. Where Jesus sent blind man to wash (Joh 9:7). Much larger than previously known Byzantine pool.
items.significance: Confirms location described in John's gospel. Large public pool with steps for immersion. Coins date it to Second Temple period. Shows John knew Jerusalem geography.

key_finds.name: Stepped pool
key_finds.details: Large rectangular pool with steps on all sides for entering water

key_finds.name: Herodian coins
key_finds.details: Coins in plaster date construction to late 1st century BC

key_finds.name: Street from Temple
key_finds.details: Pilgrimage road connecting pool to Temple Mount

items.biblical_connection: Jesus sent blind man to wash here (Joh 9:1-11). Name means 'sent' - John notes this significance. Pool fed by Gihon Spring through Hezekiah's Tunnel.
items.scholarly_notes: Discovery showed John's detailed knowledge of pre-70 AD Jerusalem. Pool destroyed with city in 70 AD, so author knew city before destruction.
items.current_location: City of David Archaeological Park, Jerusalem

items.name: Seal of Hezekiah
items.description: Clay seal impression (bulla) stamped with King Hezekiah's royal seal. Shows winged sun disk with ankh symbols. Inscription reads 'Belonging to Hezekiah [son of] Ahaz king of Judah.'
items.significance: First seal impression of Israelite or Judean king found in controlled excavation. Connects directly to one of most important kings in Bible. Shows Egyptian artistic influence.

key_finds.name: Bulla
key_finds.details: Oval seal impression, 13mm diameter, with Hebrew inscription

items.biblical_connection: Hezekiah was Judah's most faithful king (2Ki 18:5). Prepared for Assyrian invasion. Built tunnel, fortified walls. Father was wicked king Ahaz.
items.scholarly_notes: Found in fill from royal bakery area near Temple Mount. Shows administrative practices of Judean kingdom.
items.current_location: Hebrew University, Jerusalem

items.name: Dead Sea Scrolls
items.location: Found in caves near Qumran, Dead Sea
items.description: Over 900 manuscripts found in 11 caves. Include oldest copies of Hebrew Bible, community rules, and commentaries. Written in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek. Most copied; some written at Qumran.
items.significance: Pushed back oldest biblical manuscripts by 1,000 years. Show text was carefully preserved - matches later Masoretic tradition closely. Every OT book represented except Esther.

key_finds.name: Great Isaiah Scroll (1QIsa)
key_finds.details: Complete Isaiah scroll, 1,000 years older than previous oldest copy

key_finds.name: Psalms scrolls
key_finds.details: Multiple copies showing variations in psalm order

key_finds.name: Community Rule
key_finds.details: Describes beliefs and practices of Qumran community

key_finds.name: War Scroll
key_finds.details: Describes final battle between 'sons of light' and 'sons of darkness'

items.biblical_connection: Preserved text of Hebrew Bible. Isaiah 53 (suffering servant) identical to later copies. Shows scriptures Jesus read were same as ours.
items.scholarly_notes: Some scrolls match Masoretic text exactly; others show minor variations. Confirms textual reliability over millennia.
items.current_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum; some in Jordan and France

items.name: Codex Sinaiticus
items.location: Found at St. Catherine's Monastery, Sinai
items.description: One of oldest complete New Testament manuscripts. Written on vellum (animal skin) in Greek. Contains complete NT plus parts of OT and two early Christian writings. 4th century.
items.significance: Oldest complete New Testament. Shows text was stable by 4th century. Important for understanding textual history. Written by several scribes with later corrections.

key_finds.name: Complete New Testament
key_finds.details: All 27 books plus Epistle of Barnabas and Shepherd of Hermas

key_finds.name: Old Testament portions
key_finds.details: About half of Septuagint (Greek OT)

items.biblical_connection: Contains familiar text of gospels, letters, Revelation. Minor variations from later manuscripts help scholars understand copying process.
items.scholarly_notes: Story of discovery controversial - some parts may have been in monastery all along. Pages now divided between four institutions.
items.current_location: British Library (main), Leipzig University, St. Catherine's, Russian National Library

items.name: Codex Vaticanus
items.discovered: Unknown - in Vatican since at least 15th century
items.description: Possibly oldest near-complete Bible. Greek text on vellum. Missing end of Hebrews, Pastoral Epistles, Philemon, Revelation. May predate Sinaiticus slightly.
items.significance: One of two most important biblical manuscripts. Represents 'Alexandrian' text type. Very careful copying with few errors.

key_finds.name: Greek Bible
key_finds.details: Both OT (Septuagint) and most of NT in single codex

items.biblical_connection: Shows text of scripture as known in early 4th century. Basis for many modern translations along with Sinaiticus.
items.scholarly_notes: Origin unknown - possibly Egypt or Caesarea. Vatican restricted access until 19th century. Now photographed and available online.
items.current_location: Vatican Library, Rome

items.name: Rylands Papyrus P52
items.description: Oldest known New Testament manuscript fragment. Credit-card-sized piece containing John 18:31-33 (front) and 18:37-38 (back). Dialogue between Jesus and Pilate.
items.significance: Proves John's Gospel circulating in Egypt within decades of composition. Too early for legendary development. Shows rapid spread of gospel manuscripts.

key_finds.name: Fragment
key_finds.details: 8.9 x 6 cm piece with Greek text on both sides

items.biblical_connection: Contains Pilate asking 'What is truth?' and Jesus saying 'My kingdom is not of this world' (Joh 18:31-33, 37-38).
items.scholarly_notes: Dating based on handwriting style (paleography). Some scholars propose later date; consensus remains early 2nd century.
items.current_location: John Rylands Library, Manchester, UK

items.name: Nash Papyrus
items.description: Four fragments containing Ten Commandments (Exodus 20/Deuteronomy 5 combined) and Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4-5). Used for teaching or worship, not a Bible scroll.
items.significance: Oldest Hebrew biblical manuscript before Dead Sea Scrolls. Shows text was being taught and memorized. Combines two versions of Ten Commandments.

key_finds.name: Four fragments
key_finds.details: Hebrew text of Ten Commandments and Shema prayer

items.biblical_connection: Contains Ten Commandments and 'Hear O Israel, the LORD our God, the LORD is one' (Deu 6:4).
items.scholarly_notes: Text variations show liturgical use. Combination text suggests worship/teaching purpose rather than scripture scroll.
items.current_location: Cambridge University Library

items.name: Chester Beatty Papyri
items.description: Collection of 11 codices containing portions of OT, NT, and other texts. Includes early copies of Gospels, Acts, Paul's letters, and Revelation. Important for textual criticism.
items.significance: P45 contains oldest Gospels codex (c. 250 AD). P46 is oldest Pauline letters collection (c. 200 AD). P47 is oldest Revelation manuscript. Shows early codex book format.

key_finds.name: P45
key_finds.details: Portions of all four Gospels and Acts, c. 250 AD

key_finds.name: P46
key_finds.details: Most of Paul's letters including Romans, Corinthians, Galatians, c. 200 AD

key_finds.name: P47
key_finds.details: Revelation chapters 9-17, c. 280 AD

items.biblical_connection: Show NT text in 2nd-3rd century. Confirm essential accuracy of later manuscripts.
items.scholarly_notes: Some fragments held at University of Michigan (from same find). P46 missing 2 Thessalonians and Pastoral Epistles - possibly never included.
items.current_location: Chester Beatty Library, Dublin; some at University of Michigan

items.name: Bodmer Papyri
items.description: Collection of early manuscripts including P66 (John) and P75 (Luke-John). Some of earliest continuous gospel texts. Show care in copying.
items.significance: P66 dates c. 200 AD - one of oldest Gospel manuscripts. P75 (c. 175-225 AD) shows text nearly identical to 4th century Vaticanus - proving careful transmission.

key_finds.name: P66
key_finds.details: Most of John's Gospel, c. 200 AD

key_finds.name: P72
key_finds.details: Oldest 1-2 Peter and Jude, 3rd-4th century

key_finds.name: P75
key_finds.details: Most of Luke and John, c. 175-225 AD

items.biblical_connection: P66 includes John 1:1 ('In the beginning was the Word'), John 3:16, resurrection accounts.
items.scholarly_notes: P75's similarity to Vaticanus shows text remained stable for 200 years of copying.
items.current_location: Bodmer Library (Cologny, Switzerland); Vatican Library (P75 gifted in 2007)

items.name: Septuagint (LXX)
items.discovered: Always known; earliest fragments found in Qumran and Egypt
items.description: Greek translation of Hebrew Bible made for Greek-speaking Jews in Egypt. Legend says 72 translators produced identical translations (hence 'LXX'). Used by early church.
items.significance: Shows how Hebrew scripture was understood before Christ. New Testament authors often quote from Septuagint. Preserves some readings older than Masoretic text.

key_finds.name: Qumran Greek scrolls
key_finds.details: Septuagint fragments among Dead Sea Scrolls

key_finds.name: Rylands Papyrus 458
key_finds.details: Oldest Septuagint fragment (Deuteronomy), 2nd century BC

items.biblical_connection: Many NT quotations match Septuagint rather than Hebrew. Shows Jesus and apostles used Greek Bible alongside Hebrew.
items.scholarly_notes: Different books translated at different times with varying quality. Valuable for textual criticism of OT.
items.current_location: Various libraries (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus contain Septuagint)

items.name: Masoretic Text
items.discovered: Continuous tradition; Aleppo Codex (930 AD), Leningrad Codex (1008 AD)
items.description: Standard Hebrew text of Old Testament. Masoretes (Jewish scribes) added vowel marks and notes to preserve exact pronunciation and copying accuracy. Basis of modern Hebrew Bibles.
items.significance: Defines authoritative Hebrew text. Notes count letters, words, middle of books to ensure perfect copying. Dead Sea Scrolls show text was stable for 1,000 years before Masoretes.

key_finds.name: Aleppo Codex
key_finds.details: Oldest complete Masoretic manuscript (930 AD); now partially damaged

key_finds.name: Leningrad Codex
key_finds.details: Oldest complete Hebrew Bible (1008 AD); basis of modern printed editions

items.biblical_connection: Preserves text Jesus and apostles would have known. Hebrew text behind modern OT translations.
items.scholarly_notes: Masoretic notes reveal obsessive care: counts of letters, unusual spellings, middle letter of Torah.
items.current_location: Aleppo Codex in Israel Museum; Leningrad Codex in Russian National Library

items.name: Samaritan Pentateuch
items.period: c. 400 BC split from Jewish text; oldest manuscript 12th century AD
items.discovered: 1616 (Pietro della Valle brought first copy to Europe)
items.description: Samaritan version of first five books (Torah). About 6,000 differences from Masoretic text, mostly spelling. Some readings match Septuagint against Masoretic.
items.significance: Independent witness to Pentateuch text. Shows importance of Mount Gerizim to Samaritans. Some readings may preserve older forms.

key_finds.name: Abisha Scroll
key_finds.details: Samaritans' oldest scroll; tradition claims very ancient origin

key_finds.name: Qumran proto-Samaritan texts
key_finds.details: Dead Sea Scrolls showing proto-Samaritan text type

items.biblical_connection: Reflects Samaritan claim that Gerizim (not Jerusalem) is true worship site (see Joh 4:20). Jesus' conversation with Samaritan woman references this dispute.
items.scholarly_notes: Most differences trivial; major ones promote Gerizim. Split from mainstream Judaism dates to Persian or Hellenistic period.
items.current_location: Samaritan community in Holon (Israel) and Nablus; copies in various libraries

items.name: Merneptah Stele
items.description: Victory stele of Pharaoh Merneptah celebrating Libyan campaign. Final lines describe Canaan expedition including famous line: 'Israel is laid waste; its seed is no more.'
items.significance: Earliest mention of 'Israel' outside Bible. Proves Israel existed as recognized people by 1208 BC. Uses determinative showing Israel was people, not city or land.

key_finds.name: Ten-foot granite stele
key_finds.details: 28 lines of hieroglyphics; Israel mentioned in line 27

items.biblical_connection: Confirms Israel existed in Canaan by late 13th century BC. Egyptian claim of destruction is exaggerated - Israel clearly survived.
items.scholarly_notes: Israel written with 'people' determinative, not 'land' - suggests tribal confederation stage. Critical for dating Exodus and conquest.
items.current_location: Egyptian Museum, Cairo

items.name: Lachish Letters
items.description: 21 pottery fragments (ostraca) with Hebrew military correspondence. Written during Babylonian siege. Mention commanders, prophets, and watching for fire signals from Azekah.
items.significance: Snapshot of final days of Judah. One letter mentions 'we are watching for the signals of Lachish... for we cannot see Azekah' - Azekah may have already fallen.

key_finds.name: Letter 4
key_finds.details: Mentions watching for fire signals; Azekah no longer visible

key_finds.name: Letter 3
key_finds.details: Mentions unnamed prophet and army commander going to Egypt

key_finds.name: Letter 6
key_finds.details: Complaints about officials 'weakening the hands' of people

items.biblical_connection: Jeremiah mentions Lachish and Azekah as last cities standing (Jer 34:7). Officials accused Jeremiah of 'weakening the hands' of defenders (Jer 38:4) - same phrase in letters.
items.scholarly_notes: Letters show Hebrew language and military organization. References to prophet may describe Jeremiah's situation.
items.current_location: British Museum (most); Israel Museum

items.name: Arad Ostraca
items.description: Over 100 Hebrew inscriptions on pottery from Judean fortress. Administrative records including supplies, troop movements, and temple references. Name YHWH written frequently.
items.significance: Show daily administration of Judean fortress. References to 'house of YHWH' suggest temple or shrine at Arad. Names match biblical personal names (Eliashib, Nahum).

key_finds.name: Eliashib archive
key_finds.details: Letters to fortress commander about wine, flour, oil supplies

key_finds.name: Temple references
key_finds.details: Mention 'house of YHWH' and priestly families

key_finds.name: Kittim mentions
key_finds.details: Greek mercenaries receiving rations

items.biblical_connection: Name Eliashib appears in Bible (Neh 3:1, 12:10). Temple at Arad may relate to high places kings tried to remove (2Ki 18:22).
items.scholarly_notes: Arad temple with altar was decommissioned, possibly during Hezekiah's reforms. Greek mercenaries show international composition of Judean forces.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Samaria Ostraca
items.description: 63 pottery fragments recording shipments of wine and oil to palace. Name recipients, donors, places, and years. Provide insight into northern kingdom administration.
items.significance: Show tax system and royal estates. Place names match Bible (Shechem, Tirzah). Personal names contain 'Baal' element, confirming syncretism prophets condemned.

key_finds.name: Tax records
key_finds.details: Receipts for wine and oil shipments to king

key_finds.name: Place names
key_finds.details: Biblical locations including clan territories from Joshua

key_finds.name: Personal names
key_finds.details: Names with YHWH and Baal elements showing mixed worship

items.biblical_connection: Date to prosperous reign of Jeroboam II (2Ki 14:23-29). Prophets Amos and Hosea condemned northern kingdom luxury and Baal worship during this period.
items.scholarly_notes: Names with Baal element (like Abibaal, Meribaal) show continued syncretism despite official YHWH worship.
items.current_location: Istanbul Archaeological Museum

items.name: Gezer Calendar
items.description: Small limestone tablet listing agricultural seasons. Possibly a schoolboy's exercise. One of oldest Hebrew inscriptions. Lists months for plowing, planting, harvest, etc.
items.significance: Shows Hebrew writing in use during Solomon's time. Agricultural cycle matches biblical descriptions. May be mnemonic for learning calendar.

key_finds.name: Limestone tablet
key_finds.details: 10.5 x 7.2 cm with 7 lines of Hebrew

items.biblical_connection: Agricultural seasons match biblical references to plowing (1Ki 19:19), harvest (Rut 2:23), grape gathering (Jdg 9:27). From Solomon's fortified city.
items.scholarly_notes: Interpretation debated - months or activities? Writing style suggests early Hebrew script development.
items.current_location: Istanbul Archaeological Museum

items.name: YHWH Inscriptions
items.location: Various sites including Kuntillet Ajrud and Khirbet el-Qom
items.description: Ancient Hebrew inscriptions containing the divine name YHWH. Some mention 'YHWH of Samaria,' 'YHWH of Teman,' and controversially 'YHWH and his Asherah.'
items.significance: Show widespread use of divine name. 'YHWH and his Asherah' texts confirm prophets' condemnations of syncretism - people worshipped YHWH alongside goddess.

key_finds.name: Kuntillet Ajrud inscriptions
key_finds.details: Blessing texts mentioning 'YHWH of Samaria and his Asherah'

key_finds.name: Khirbet el-Qom inscription
key_finds.details: Tomb blessing mentioning YHWH and possibly Asherah

items.biblical_connection: Confirms prophetic condemnation of Asherah poles alongside YHWH worship (1Ki 14:15, 2Ki 17:16, 2Ki 23:6). Shows popular religion included goddess worship.
items.scholarly_notes: Debate over 'Asherah' - goddess, cult object, or feminine aspect? Inscriptions prove syncretism was real problem prophets addressed.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Ekron Inscription
items.description: Five-line inscription identifying site as Ekron and naming rulers. Mentions Achish son of Padi - Padi is known from Assyrian records as king of Ekron restored by Sennacherib.
items.significance: First inscription definitively identifying a Philistine city. Confirms site identification. Names match Assyrian records. Shows Philistine writing in Semitic language.

key_finds.name: Temple dedication
key_finds.details: Inscription dedicating temple to goddess Ptgyh (possibly Pythogeia)

items.biblical_connection: Ekron was one of five Philistine cities (Jos 13:3). Achish may be related to king of Gath who sheltered David (1Sa 27:2). Padi mentioned in Sennacherib's annals.
items.scholarly_notes: Longest Philistine inscription found. Shows cultural assimilation - Philistines adopted Semitic language while keeping Greek goddess names.
items.current_location: Israel Museum, Jerusalem

items.name: Deir Alla Inscription (Balaam Inscription)
items.location: Found at Tell Deir Alla, Jordan
items.description: Plaster inscription found fallen from wall. Mentions 'Balaam son of Beor' as 'seer of the gods.' Contains prophecy of divine council deciding destruction. Written in dialect related to Hebrew.
items.significance: First reference to biblical figure outside Bible that's not a king. Confirms Balaam was remembered centuries later. Shows he was famous prophet in region.

key_finds.name: Plaster fragments
key_finds.details: 119 fragments with black and red ink text

items.biblical_connection: Balaam son of Beor was seer hired by Moab to curse Israel (Num 22-24). Instead blessed Israel. Later led Israel into sin (Num 31:16). Remembered negatively in NT (2Pe 2:15, Jud 1:11).
items.scholarly_notes: Text shows Balaam remembered as legitimate prophet in Transjordan. Confirms biblical presentation of him as real historical figure.
items.current_location: Jordan Archaeological Museum, Amman
